title: Stargazy pie
subtitle: The Cornish pilchard pie with fish heads peering through the pastry lid
excerpt: The famous Cornish fish pie in which the heads of whole pilchards or sardines protrude through the pastry lid, looking up at the stars. Made annually in Mousehole on Tom Bawcock's Eve. A pie as much about occasion as flavour.
sourceNotes: Stargazy pie is documented as a Cornish festival dish connected to the legend of Tom Bawcock, a fisherman from Mousehole who is said to have saved the village from starvation by sailing out in a storm on 23rd December (Tom Bawcock's Eve) and returning with a boatful of fish. The pie is documented in Cornish food history and in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, as a regional tradition with the heads-through-pastry method as its defining characteristic.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made and refrigerated a day ahead. Assemble and bake on the day of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A crumbly, plain pastry made by rubbing fat into flour until it resembles breadcrumbs, then binding with cold water. Used for pie cases and tart shells.

content.text: Stargazy pie is a Cornish tradition as much as a recipe. The dish is made each year in the village of Mousehole on the 23rd of December to commemorate Tom Bawcock, a local fisherman who is said to have braved a severe winter storm to bring back a catch that saved the village from famine. The defining feature of the pie, whole pilchards or sardines with their heads protruding through the
lid and gazing skyward, is intentional theatre. The heads allow the natural fish oils to run back into the filling rather than being lost in cooking.

The filling is a simple combination of whole fish laid over a bed of egg, bacon, and potato, bound with a light cream sauce. The pastry lid is laid over the filled dish with the fish heads pushed through cuts made in the pastry before the final seal.

attrs.prepNote: cold, for the pastry; or 175 g all-butter
3 hard-boiled and sliced for the filling; 1 beaten for the egg wash

content.text: Rub {{lard}} and {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add 5–6 tablespoons of cold water and bring together to a firm dough. Divide into two-thirds and one-third. Wrap both pieces and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Clean and gut 6 whole fresh sardines or pilchards, leaving the heads attached. Season the inside of each fish with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and a few drops of {{lemon}} juice. Mix {{double-cream}} with a little more lemon juice to taste. Hard-boil three of the {{eggs}}, cool, and slice. Scatter the sliced eggs over the base of a deep oval pie dish about 28 cm long. Lay the fish over the eggs, arranged so the tails point towards the centre and the heads will emerge from the edges. Pour the cream mixture over the filling.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Roll the larger piece of pastry to cover the pie dish. Make six cuts in the pastry where the fish heads will emerge. Lower the pastry over the dish, threading the fish heads through the cuts. Press the edges to seal and crimp. Brush with beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 35–40 minutes until the pastry is deeply golden and the fish is cooked through. Serve hot.
Pilchards (mature sardines) are the traditional fish. Fresh sardines are more readily available and work well; herring is a suitable substitute.
The pie is traditionally eaten on Tom Bawcock's Eve (23rd December) in Mousehole, Cornwall. Making it at any other time of year is entirely acceptable.
The fish oils that run from the exposed heads during baking enrich the cream filling — this is the practical reason for the dramatic presentation.
